title: Built three trading systems- Scans, executes, manages risk autonomously. Used Public API Openclaw skill
author: BuildwithPublic
selftext:
Would love to hear if someone has done something similar and executing live now. Curious about your journey to get there. Happy to share mine. 

I run three engines in parallel -one trading micro futures, one trading ETF options, plus an SPY 0DTE scalper that fires when ES hits extremes. Fully autonomous.

The core model uses proprietary chaos theory and regime filtering. Phase-space reconstruction turns raw price into a multi-dimensional system, that measures the energy behind a move - trend accelerating flat or dying? That signal decides everything: what to trade, which direction, what strategy to use. For options it picks the structure too- spreads, condors, naked, whatever fits the regime.

Risk management is built in-  Per-trade limits, portfolio-wide exposure caps, daily circuit breaker that shuts everything down if I hit my pain point. 0DTE positions get auto-killed before close. Every alert hits my phone in real time.

comments.author: ja_trader
comments.body: i will be once i get chatgpt to explain it to me!

replies.author: BuildwithPublic
replies.body: Codex is solid, I found Opus 4.6 to be a bit better for strat context. Used Public API openclaw skill- pretty easy to get running, worked around some plumbing issues for a week or so, but got live quick.

replies.author: safsoft
replies.body: interesting, can you please give more details about you workflow and pipeline ?

replies.author: BuildwithPublic
replies.body: Sure I’ll DM you

comments.author: ValuableSleep9175
comments.body:
I am in the research phase right now.  I have multiple ideas that my pipeline tests, along with a baseline and a regime awareness variant.  It trains on 1.5 years of data.  I have a script that runs through every idea and market regime.  Ideas are separate files from the code so I can just add more as I go.

I have a backtester that runs the top ideas through different exit strategies. 

A promotion model that picks the best back test and feeds that to my trader, with the settings from the machine learning entry and the back tested exits. 

It is very early on but the end goal is to generate a few new ideas a week. Run them through the pipeline and promote the best ideas automatically. 

I still need to start some risk management, right now it is basic just x% of portfolio per trade.  Paper trading atm.

Lot of chatGPT and codex.  I need to understand a little better all the strats that have been built.  Momentum, mean revision I get, but we made 20 some ideas.  Still a bit fuzzy on the math/machine learning part.

replies.author: Forward_Detail3004
replies.body:
Hey there
I’ve been trading for over 8 years, but I don’t have experience with programming or AI — I’m more of an old-school trader.

I’d really love to turn my strategy into an algorithm / EA one day so it can trade automatically. I still enjoy taking one trade per week manually, but having an automated system would be amazing.

Is there anyone here who could help make this happen or point me in the right direction?
Thanks in advance!

replies.author: ValuableSleep9175
replies.body:
I didn't know anything from anything.  A lot of chatting with chatGPT.  Now I use codex locally on my machine to read my codebase and make updates.  Heavy coding with the Web interface was bad.  Too much amnesia.  Codex just reads everything and is tons better. 

Good luck.

replies.author: No_Fox9998
replies.body: How is this integrated with a broker like IB or RH or ETrade?

replies.author: BuildwithPublic
replies.body: The Openclaw skill is specifically designed to execute on the Public brokerage API. It translates natural language trade intent into structured Public API order calls -execution happens through Public. Not broker-agnostic, purpose-built for Public’s execution stack.

replies.author: No_Fox9998
replies.body: Didn't realize [Public.com](http://Public.com) is a brokerage :),

comments.author: AlbatiQue
comments.body:
Looks really interesting. Do you trust openclaw with this ?
How are the returns so far?
Do you run that locally?

replies.author: BuildwithPublic
replies.body:
Just wiring around very well defined deterministic rules. The risk parameters are explicit and position sizing is controlled. 

Returns really depend on whatever strategy you want to create- I just went live about 2 weeks ago from some strats that I used manually for years. 

 I run it locally. Execution and API keys stay on my machine.

replies.author: wow_98

replies.author: BuildwithPublic
replies.body: The dashboard just shows what the models are “thinking” about and how they see the best trade to enter or which strategy it should run. Then it places those trades autonomously via the API. Three discrete strategies, rolled up into one aggregate portfolio.

replies.author: wow_98

comments.author: HeyItsYourDad_AMA
comments.body: This is cool. Has it been successful?

replies.author: BuildwithPublic
replies.body: I took profitable strats that I’ve used for years on options and futures. worked through them on the public api openclaw skill with Claude/codex/gemini to get everything fully automated. Definitely some bumps and you just gotta have a little bit of patience and persistence. But the execution layer is really solid now. It’s truly incredible what you can do with these tools now

comments.author: wow_98
comments.body: On what basis do you have it trade futures ?

replies.author: BuildwithPublic
replies.body: You mean frequency?

replies.author: wow_98
replies.body: I meant logic and frequency

replies.author: BuildwithPublic
replies.body: Logic is multi-factor momentum scoring, execution engine is pretty simple on anchored means. Either buy/sell/flat regime detected, then execution runs with risk parameters and guardrails built in. Rescores every 8hrs, assigned regime, executes.

comments.author: Double0J
comments.body: Are you a profitable discretionary trader or algorithmic trader before making these? Just curious.

replies.author: BuildwithPublic
replies.body: From an active trader perspective, paid a lot of tuition to the mkt before I found a style that suited my personality. Built around my weaknesses, then I paid some more tuition to the mkt. I discovered my ultimate weakness was the execution piece from a discretionary lens. I needed to take my brain out of the equation, for the most part. So I took what I knew worked and turned it into a deterministic and repeatable path. This was the turning point.

replies.body: Really interesting, thanks

comments.author: russobarriga
comments.body: I'm running something conceptually similar, but with a different architecture. Instead of a single core model, I use a probabilistic forecasting layer built on 11+ ML models to produce distribution-aware price forecasts rather than directional signals. Those forecasts are fed into a context layer that combines price, fundamental and technical streams. Final execution decisions aren’t rule-based — they’re handled by an LLM decision layer that evaluates regime and risk management before placing trades. Everything runs live on Hyperliquid with real capital.

comments.author: Busy-Specialist7708
comments.body:
Interesting! Well, I build an operating FX trading desk in OpenAI's projects, based on a macro regime flow framework I designed. I've build in quite some solid risk filters and score mechanisms. I have several desks that focus on specific things, like a quant desk, TA desk, operater desk, R&D desk and they provide me with a regime dashboard, reports and peer reviews, but exclusively funneled through my macro regime framework, no AI discretionary thinking allowed. Still have a big to do list, but that is basically refining and converting. Yesterday I provided a huge update to the desk, which pushed it to the final stage and is now able to live test. Currently foward testing papermoney and the results are amazingly good. It gave me 3 tier A setups for this week, but to avoid stacked risk I'm in 2 trades now and both are in the greenzone, one even close at reaching initial target.
I've partnered up with somebody who is more tech savvy then me. I'm an old school manual trader. If all is well, we gonna program it into an autonomous agent. That's the end goal 🦾

comments.author: Disastrous_Hotel_574
comments.body:
The setup with OpenClaw is impressive. This is exactly the direction trading is taking in 2026: total autonomy driven by agents.

I have a tool for that if you want to try it: [https://hercul-market-web.vercel.app/](https://hercul-market-web.vercel.app/)

For my part, I took a complementary approach with Hercul Market. Rather than pure chaos theory, I focused on automating Market Structure (SMC) via AI to replace the Excel factories that can no longer handle multi-stream loads.

A few technical points that interest me in your architecture:

Regime Filtering: How do you manage the latency of your OpenClaw skill on the execution of 0DTE options during volatility spikes?

Circuit Breaker: I implemented a similar system via PostgreSQL/Supabase to lock access in case of excessive drawdown. It's the only way to keep your IRR in the green over the long term.

Data Sync: Do you link your logs to a proprietary dashboard or do you stick with the raw console?

The hardest part isn't building the system, it's trusting it when the regime changes abruptly. If you want to see how I visualize the market structure to validate my models, the link is just above.

I'm curious to learn more about your phase-space reconstruction management! 📈

Respect the build.

How are you handling regime shifts and correlation spikes?   
  
I ask because that’s usually where autonomous systems get properly tested.

replies.body: Great question, vol spikes are the big test right now I think I can handle this on hedging side. correlations are handled pretty well via model hedging.

comments.author: MaximKiselev
comments.body: its cool but where sre results? Are you not reaching your LLM limits with frequent trading? Autonomous agents aren't much different from regular bots. They're just wrapped in a different packaging. Most people who develop such systems then sell courses, although in reality, if the system works, why bother? Overall, I'm a skeptic; I've seen bots lose fortunes more often than they've made any money. In reality, buy and hold always works better.

replies.author: BuildwithPublic
replies.body: Skepticism is healthy

comments.author: ok-hacker
comments.body: Love the chaos theory approach to regime detection. We use similar phase-space analysis on andmilo for Solana trades—the energy behind moves is key. Congrats on going live with strategies you've traded manually for years.

replies.author: BuildwithPublic
replies.body: That’s awesome I’ve never seen anyone else use it. Thanks are you doing anything outside of solana?

replies.author: ok-hacker
replies.body: Solana-focused for now - the speed and low fees make it the right chain for the strategies milo runs. But perpetuals on other chains is on the roadmap, hyperliquid specifically. Your systems covering futures and equities in parallel is impressive, curious how you're handling the regime differences across asset classes.
